title: Spiritual Bypass Bingo Card
dek: You know that tightness in your masseter muscle at 3 a.m., the way your diaphragm refuses to soften when the world demands you rise and shine, the subtle but relentless grip of the levator scapulae when you try to sit still. This is not pathology, this is biology. Here’s a way to recognize the dance of avoidance in your body and mind  -  a Spiritual Bypass Bingo Card that honors your fury and the wisdom held in your fascia.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Jaw Remembers: Masseter Muscle and the Quiet Denial


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.972Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s three in the morning, the world outside is draped in a heavy fog that smells faintly of damp leaves and distant smoke. Your masseter muscle clenches, a silent sentinel guarding a secret you’re not yet ready to face. This muscle, responsible for the simple act of chewing, carries a history of words unspoken, emotions swallowed whole. The tightness is not a failure of will but a biological record of your attempts to bypass the rawness beneath your skin. Pema Chödrön teaches us that leaning into discomfort rather than sidestepping it is the path to true presence. Yet, here you are, jaw clenched tight, the fascia around it taut like the strings of a violin tuned to a note of avoidance.

This is the essence of what some call “spiritual bypass.” It’s not just a mental game; it’s written in your body’s soft tissues and connective layers. The masseter doesn’t just clench when you’re angry; it tightens when you push your fury aside, when you tell yourself to forgive and forget before you’re ready, when you mask pain with platitudes. Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic psychotherapist, reminds us that the body never lies. It holds the truth of your fury in every fiber. When you soften that muscle, when you let go of the clenched jaw, you invite the story beneath to surface, to breathe, to be witnessed.

## The Diaphragm’s Silent Rebellion

At dawn, when the first light filters through gauzy curtains, your diaphragm refuses to expand fully. It’s a subtle rebellion, a tightening that feels like a whisper in your lungs, a hesitation in the breath that should be effortless. The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your ribs, is your primary breath engine. When it contracts fully, it massages your heart, your liver, your stomach; it orchestrates the rhythm of life itself. But when it constricts, the breath becomes shallow, the body braces, and the mind follows suit, eager to avoid the full spectrum of feeling.

Gabor Maté’s work on trauma and embodied truth resonates here. He speaks of how early wounds embed themselves in the body’s core, how the diaphragm can become a holding place for unprocessed grief and anger. You might not have words for it at 5 a.m., but your body knows. The diaphragm’s resistance is a form of spiritual bypass  -  a subtle but powerful mechanism to avoid the ache of your own fury. It’s not weakness; it’s survival. Herbs like [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support this muscle’s release, but the true work is in the willingness to let the breath deepen, to feel the discomfort of rage without flinching.

## The Levator Scapulae Holds the Weight of Unseen Burdens

As the day progresses and the sky turns a muted gray, the levator scapulae muscle  -  that small but fierce muscle running from your cervical vertebrae to your shoulder blades  -  tightens in a familiar pattern. This muscle is the body’s whisperer of tension, the keeper of burdens you won’t name aloud. It aches with the weight of silent fury, the unacknowledged resentment that simmers beneath forced smiles and polite conversation.

Resmaa Menakem’s teachings on racialized trauma and somatic healing highlight how this muscle, like others, carries more than personal history; it holds collective pain. The levator scapulae’s chronic constriction is a map of spiritual bypass in action  -  a refusal to fully inhabit your anger because it feels unsafe or unacceptable. Yet, this muscle’s tension also signals a yearning for truth, a somatic plea to acknowledge what has been denied. Working with the levator scapulae requires more than stretching; it demands presence, a gentle but unwavering attention to what your body is shouting beneath the noise of spiritual platitudes.

In these muscles and their fascia, you find the real story  -  not one of failure but of survival, of wisdom encoded in flesh and bone. As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, healing is about reclaiming the body’s narrative, not overriding it with empty words. The Spiritual Bypass Bingo Card is not a game to win or lose; it’s a mirror to your embodied truth, a map to the fury you were taught to hide but that your body remembers with fierce clarity.

## The Jaw Remembers: Masseter Muscle and the Quiet Lie of "Just Be"

You feel it first in your jaw. The masseter tightens, a slow, insistent knot beneath the skin, as if your body is whispering the truth you’re trying to drown out. It’s just past midnight, the room is heavy with the scent of dry chamomile and a faint trace of sandalwood incense, your trapezius muscles pull at the base of your neck, and still, you tell yourself to "just be." Thich Nhat Hanh’s gentle urgings echo in your mind - "Be here now." But what if being here now doesn’t mean sitting still in passive acceptance? What if it means moving through the layers of held tension, the clenched masseter that guards the doorway to your fury?

The phrase "just be" can become a trap - a spiritual bypass that glosses over the visceral reality of your body’s messages. You learned early that your anger was a problem, a wrinkle in the otherwise smooth fabric of your relationships. So you press your tongue against the roof of your mouth, trying to will the masseter to relax, but the fascia around it remains taut, stubborn. This is not pathology; this is biology. Robert Augustus Masters reminds us that the jaw is a site of profound emotional holding. It remembers the times you bit down hard to stop a scream, the moments you clenched your teeth to keep the rage from spilling out. This muscle is a ledger of your fury, each fiber a line item in the story you’ve been taught to deny.

In this moment, you might try an embodied practice: Place your fingertips gently on your masseter as you inhale deeply through your nose, feeling the diaphragm expand downward, the belly pushing soft against your hands. On the exhale, let your jaw soften slightly, maybe even part your teeth just a fraction. Notice the shift - not just in the muscle but in the subtle opening in your experience. The jaw remembers, yes, but it also knows how to release, how to surrender the tight narrative of "I must be calm" to the wild, raw truth of your body’s message. As Karla McLaren teaches, acknowledging anger with respect and curiosity is the first step toward true somatic liberation.

## Psoas and the Weight of Denial: Embodied Resistance to the "Everything Happens for a Reason" Mantra

The psoas, deep within your core, anchors your body to the earth. It wraps around your lumbar spine, connecting your torso to your legs, a powerful muscle that responds to your emotional climate. When you hear "everything happens for a reason," your psoas contracts subtly, like a shield against the flood of unprocessed feelings. This contraction is not a passive tightening - it’s a whole-body reaction to a phrase that often invalidates your lived experience.

At 3 A.M., when the world is silent except for the faint rustle of wind through the trees outside, you might lie on your back, feeling the cool sheets against your skin, your psoas muscles stiff and unyielding. This phrase, often repeated in spiritual circles, can feel like a slap to the nervous system, a dismissal of your right to rage, grief, or confusion. Gabor Maté’s work on trauma teaches us that the body holds the unspoken stories, the silent screams embedded in muscle and fascia. Your psoas is no exception; it holds the tension of your denied fury, your suppressed "why me?"

To work with this, try a somatic exploration: Lie down with your knees bent, feet flat on the bed. Place your hands on your lower abdomen, just above the pelvis. As you breathe in, imagine the breath reaching the psoas, coaxing it to soften. On the exhale, visualize the muscle releasing its grip, melting into the support beneath you. Don’t force it; let the breath be the guide. This is a conversation with your body, not a command.

Resmaa Menakem’s teachings on racialized trauma emphasize how the body archives collective and personal pain. Your psoas is a keeper of these stories, and when you allow its release, you’re not just healing yourself - you’re participating in a lineage of bodily remembering and restoration. The mantra "everything happens for a reason" becomes less a cage and more a whispered invitation to sit with your anger, to honor it as a messenger rather than a mistake.

## The Levator Scapulae’s Silent Scream: Weathering the "Good Vibes Only" Storm

The levator scapulae muscles, nestled at the back and side of your neck, lift the scapulae and often bear the brunt of emotional strain. They throb quietly beneath your fingertips, especially on gray, rainy mornings when the air is thick with moisture and your skin carries the faint musk of earth and rain-dampened herbs like valerian root. When you hear "good vibes only," your levator scapulae stiffen, a physical manifestation of the emotional labor of hiding your true feelings.

Pema Chödrön’s teachings on embracing discomfort come alive here. She encourages sitting with the edges of your experience, the sharpness of your fury, without flinching or fleeing. But "good vibes only" feels like a mandate to flee, to cover the ache with a forced smile. Your levator scapulae tighten in protest, pulling your shoulder blades upward, compressing the cervical spine. This tension can cause headaches, a throbbing at the base of the skull, as if your body is screaming through the muscles you’ve been told to keep silent.

In these moments, herbs can be allies. A cup of lemon balm tea, its bright, citrusy aroma filling the room, can soothe the nervous system, helping the levator scapulae to unclench. The practice of mindful movement - slow, deliberate neck rolls or gentle shoulder shrugs - can further coax the muscles to relax. Bessel van der Kolk’s insights on trauma remind us that the body’s holding patterns are not weaknesses but survival strategies. To soften the levator scapulae is to tell your body it’s safe to lower its guard.

Try this now: With your eyes closed, inhale deeply, imagining the breath flowing into the tight spaces between your shoulder blades. Exhale slowly, allowing the shoulders to drop away from your ears. Notice the weight of the rain outside, the way it mirrors the heaviness in your muscles. Let yourself feel the tension without judgment. This is not failure; this is the body’s wisdom speaking through the language of muscle and fascia.

---

In these muscles - the masseter, the psoas, the levator scapulae - your anger is alive, a message encoded in tissue and breath. The bypass phrases you’ve learned are like static on the line, obscuring the clarity of your body’s voice. By tuning into these specific sites of tension, by honoring the teachers who illuminate the path, you reclaim your fury as a vital, unashamed messenger. The jaw remembers. The psoas holds the story. The levator scapulae cry out in silence. And you, with your breath and your herbs, listen deeply.

## The Jaw Remembers: Unlocking the Masseter and Levator Scapulae

You might not realize it when the world is spinning under the heavy, humid cloak of a July night, but your body is a map of all the bypasses you've ever taken. The masseter muscle, that dense, tireless workhorse clenching and flexing along your jawline, holds grudges you forgot you had. It tightens, knotted beneath the skin, as if trying to muffle the sound of your own fury. This is not pathology; this is biology. The fascia surrounding it, thick and unyielding, binds the muscle fibers in a slow, suffocating embrace. When you grind your teeth in the late-night quiet, it's not just stress - it’s the body refusing to let the anger be lost in spiritual platitudes.

Your levator scapulae, that small muscle at the nape of your neck responsible for elevating your shoulder blades, stiffens as you silently swallow yet another “all is well.” Its fascia, woven into the trapezius and rhomboid complex, stiffens with every attempt to spiritualize away your discomfort. Karla McLaren’s teachings remind us that emotions are information, not enemies; they insist you listen to what your body is saying, even when your mind wants to dress it up in pretty words. The tightness in your neck and jaw is a protest, a somatic shout that the bypass is failing. When you inhale, notice how your diaphragm struggles against the constricted fascia, how the breath feels caught in the soft tissues, how the scent of your herbal salve - perhaps a balm infused with lavender and frankincense - reminds you that healing is a physical act, not a mental one.

## The psoas and the Haunting Weight of Avoidance

The psoas muscle, that deep core muscle anchoring your spine to your legs, is the keeper of your fight-or-flight history. It remembers the nights when anger was dangerous to express, when fury was a sin whispered between the cracks of your family’s dinning room walls. Its fascia, thick and fibrous, holds the tension of avoided rage like a ledger marked with unpaid debts. Robert Augustus Masters describes this as the 'body's emotional memory,' a vivid archive of survival strategies etched into muscle.

You can feel it if you lie on your back just before dawn, when the world is still draped in a blue-gray quiet, and the first birds dare to sing. There, in the subtle tremble of the psoas, you find the resistance to your own awakening. This is where Resmaa Menakem’s work becomes crucial - his reminder that the body itself carries the trauma, that you must sink into the sensation to truly begin to release. And release does not come from positive affirmations or chasing lightness; it comes from the raw, unashamed acknowledgment of the heaviness you carry.

The smell of earth after rain and a cup of bittersweet wormwood tea, steeped long enough to turn deep amber, help you ground into this somatic truth. Herbs are not trite prescriptions; they are allies in your reckoning. The bitter notes open your digestion and your nervous system, allowing the psoas to soften, the fascia to untangle, the body to remember that anger is not a failure but a signal. Pema Chödrön’s wisdom flows here: the path to liberation is not through avoidance but through leaning into discomfort, holding it with kindness, not bypass.

## FAQ

**Q: How can I tell if I’m engaging in spiritual bypassing without realizing it?**

**A:** The body never lies. If you notice persistent tension in your muscles - like a clenched masseter, a tight levator scapulae, or a braced psoas - especially during moments when you’re telling yourself to just "stay positive" or "send love," you might be bypassing. These physical sensations are your body's way of saying, "You’re not done with this yet." Teachers like Gabor Maté emphasize listening to these signals as essential to true healing.

**Q: What practical steps can I take to honor my anger through the body?**

**A:** Begin by bringing your attention to specific muscles that hold tension - the jaw, neck, or core. Use your breath to soften the diaphragm and invite movement into these areas. Somatic practices, such as gentle stretching or simply placing a hand over the tight spot, can help. Karla McLaren suggests acknowledging the emotion as a messenger, asking it what it needs rather than trying to banish it.

**Q: Can herbs really help with emotional processing?**

**A:** Yes, herbs are powerful allies. They work on the physical body, which is where emotions are stored. For instance, nervines like skullcap or lemon balm can soothe the nervous system, making it easier to sit with difficult feelings without becoming overwhelmed. They don't erase the anger; they provide the somatic support needed to process it.

**Q: Why is it so hard to just "let go" of anger?**

**A:** Because anger is often a protective response to boundary violations or unmet needs. When you try to "let go" without addressing the underlying issue, the body resists. The fascia tightens, and the muscles brace. As Robert Augustus Masters points out, true release comes from moving through the emotion, not skipping over it.

**Q: How do I differentiate between healthy anger and destructive rage?**

**A:** Healthy anger is clear, focused, and often accompanied by a sense of groundedness, even if it's intense. It moves through the body and leads to action or boundary-setting. Destructive rage, on the other hand, often feels chaotic, overwhelming, and disconnected from the present moment. It’s usually a sign that older, unprocessed trauma is being triggered. Somatic awareness helps you stay present with the anger so it doesn't spiral into rage.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.972Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on spiritual bypass bingo card, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
